parentguardian_information.mailing_address: 1247 Aspen Meadow Drive, Fort Collins, CO 80526

participant_background.previous_outdoor_experience_skill_level:
I’ve grown up in Colorado and have been doing various outdoor activities with my family since I was about 7. Most of my experience is day hiking and car camping, with a few beginner-level backpacking trips. I would describe my overall outdoor skill level as somewhere between beginner and intermediate.

Hiking: I typically go on day hikes at least once or twice a month when the weather is good, mostly in places like Horsetooth Mountain Open Space, Lory State Park, and Rocky Mountain National Park. My usual distance is 4–8 miles round trip with moderate elevation gain (1,000–1,800 feet). I’m comfortable carrying a small daypack (around 10–15 lbs) with water, snacks, a light jacket, and basic supplies. I know how to pace myself on uphill sections, take short water breaks, and manage my energy so I don’t burn out too early.

Camping: I’ve gone car camping with my family most summers, usually 2–3 trips per year. We’ve stayed at campgrounds like Moraine Park in RMNP and State Forest State Park. I know how to help set up a basic dome tent, roll and pack a sleeping bag, and set up my sleeping pad. I can help collect small sticks and kindling for campfires (where allowed), and I know basic fire safety like keeping water nearby, not leaving a fire unattended, and making sure the fire is completely out before going to bed. I’m used to sleeping in a tent, using a headlamp at night, and dealing with nighttime noises.

Backpacking: I’ve done two short backpacking trips so far, both 1 night/2 days. One was in the Rawah Wilderness with my dad and uncle, where we hiked about 5 miles in with full packs (mine was around 20–22 lbs). I helped filter water using a squeeze filter, learned how to hang a bear bag with help, and used a small backpacking stove to boil water for freeze-dried meals. I’m still learning how to pack my bag efficiently and adjust the straps so the weight sits on my hips instead of my shoulders, but I’ve gotten better on each trip.

Navigation & safety: I know basic map and trail etiquette, like staying on marked trails, not cutting switchbacks, and following Leave No Trace principles (pack it in, pack it out; respect wildlife; camp on durable surfaces). I can read simple trail maps and understand trail junction signs, but I’m not yet comfortable navigating completely off-trail or with just a compass. I carry a whistle and know the general idea of staying put if lost, making noise, and trying to be visible.

Weather & clothing: I’m used to Colorado’s quickly changing weather. I know to bring layers (base layer, fleece, and rain shell) and I usually hike in synthetic or wool socks instead of cotton to avoid blisters. I have decent waterproof hiking boots that are already broken in. I’ve hiked in light rain, snow flurries in the fall, and summer heat, and I know the importance of staying hydrated and watching out for signs of heat exhaustion or getting too cold.

Other skills: I’m comfortable around camp stoves and can safely light and turn off a small backpacking stove under supervision. I can help with simple camp chores like washing dishes in a designated area, packing food into bear boxes where provided, and keeping my gear organized. I’m not an expert at anything yet, but I’m eager to learn and I take instructions seriously. I’m good at working with groups, following safety rules, and asking questions if I’m unsure about something.
participant_background.physical_emotional_behavioral_characteri:
Physically, I’m generally healthy and active, but there are a few things that would be helpful for staff to know so they can support me.

Asthma: I have mild, exercise-induced asthma that mostly shows up when I’m doing intense activity at higher elevations or in cold, dry air. It’s well controlled with an inhaler (albuterol). I don’t usually have problems on regular hikes if I pace myself, but if we’re doing a steep climb or moving quickly, I may need to stop and use my inhaler and take a few deep breaths. My asthma has never required hospitalization, but I have had to sit out of soccer practice a couple of times in middle school when I pushed too hard. My parents and I are very used to managing it.

Allergies: I have seasonal allergies (pollen, grasses) that can make me sneezy and give me itchy eyes, especially in late spring and early summer. I normally take a non-drowsy antihistamine in the morning during allergy season. I don’t have any known food allergies or anaphylactic reactions, and I do not carry an EpiPen.

Endurance & fitness: I’m reasonably fit for my age—I play JV soccer at school and also ride my bike around town—but I sometimes start out too fast and then need to slow down. If we’re doing a long uphill section, I do better if I can set a steady but moderate pace rather than trying to keep up with the very fastest people. Short, regular water breaks really help me. I can comfortably hike with a backpack for several hours, but I may get quiet when I’m tired and focus more on just walking.

Emotional/behavioral: I’m generally easygoing and get along well with peers and adults. I tend to be a little shy at first in new groups, especially if I don’t know anyone, but I open up after a day or two. I really appreciate clear instructions and knowing the plan for the day (for example, approximate hiking distance, elevation gain, and what time we’re aiming to reach camp). If plans change suddenly, I’m usually fine, but I do better if someone explains why the change is happening.

Anxiety: I have mild situational anxiety, especially around heights and narrow exposed trails. I’m not paralyzed by it, but if we’re on a steep drop-off or a very narrow ledge, I may slow down a lot and feel nervous. It helps me if a staff member can stay relatively close by, talk me through where to place my feet, and remind me to focus on the trail rather than looking over the edge. I haven’t had a panic attack on a hike before, but I have felt my heart race and my hands get sweaty on exposed sections. Taking a few deep breaths, pausing briefly, and having calm reassurance really helps.

Socially: I’m not usually the loudest or most talkative person in a group, but I’m kind, cooperative, and I try to be supportive of others. I don’t like a lot of yelling or teasing, even if it’s meant as a joke, and I may withdraw a bit if there’s a lot of aggressive joking or loud conflict. I respond best to calm, respectful communication and encouragement rather than being called out in front of everyone.

Sleep & routines: I sometimes have trouble falling asleep the first night in a new place (including in tents). Wearing a beanie and having my own sleeping bag and pillowcase from home helps me feel more comfortable. I might be a little quieter or groggier in the morning if I didn’t sleep well, but I usually perk up after breakfast and some movement. I don’t snore loudly according to my family, and I don’t sleepwalk.

Overall, I am very excited about being outdoors and I’m motivated to participate fully. I follow rules, I’m open to feedback, and I try to be considerate of how my actions affect the group. If staff notice that I’m getting very quiet or hanging back more than usual, it might mean I’m feeling anxious, overwhelmed, or extra tired, and it usually helps if someone checks in with me one-on-one to see how I’m doing.
